But I didn’t care. 

I wrote my book.

You could call it, “The Psychology of Eating.”
We ended up calling it, “You Can’t Screw This Up.”

Along the way, I realized I wanted to write another book. 

But it would be an uphill battle.

I wanted to discuss how most diets break people mentally and that leads them to fail physically. 

The problem: most publishers don’t want that book. 

They want the quick fix.

11 years ago I wrote a New York Times bestseller.
And then I walked away. 

Behind that decision was a discomfort about the direction of wellness. 

A trend to push an extreme version of dieting, trading quick results for long-term failure and frustration. 

So I took a break.

Speaking with certainty about uncertain outcomes --- based on unproven principles -- is peak wellness BS. 

People struggle to eat well, sleep enough, move daily, and connect socially. This is where we must focus to truly help.

Instead, we give attention to biohacking bullshit. https://t.co/fl5fcd4Qgx

My father is gone. 

There are no losers when you fight cancer. 

But there are many lessons for those of us who still get more time.

@Schwarzenegger gave me the ultimate honor and let me share how my dad turned a death sentence into a life sentence. 

https://t.co/gwjDVseUs3

At 65 years old, my dad was given a death sentence. But a funny thing happened.

Instead of waiting for death, my dad leaned into optimism and got busy living.

Death tapped my dad on the shoulder, but he gave the grim reaper the middle finger.

And my dad did the impossible.

Dad: He should put a shirt on, get out of the tub, and just do something instead of talking so much. Since when is sitting in the cold an accomplishment worth showing? People will do anything for attention and pretend it’s to help others. 

Amen, dad. Going to miss you.

My dad: Is he in a bathtub?

Me: it’s a cold tub

Dad: what’s the difference?

Me: It keeps the water really cold and it’s $10k.

Dad: A $10k bathtub. What’s he talking about?

Me: motivation 

Dad: and people watch this?

Me: more than you’d believe

My father passed away last Friday. He left me with many lessons in his final weeks.

Before he lost his vision, he saw me scrolling on social media. 

He saw a guy talking in a cold tub. And my dads reaction was priceless…

Slow down. 

Research on more than 60,000 people published in the BMJ suggests that slow eaters are 42 percent less likely to suffer from obesity. 

When you take your time (20 to 30 min), it increases the hormones that promote fullness. 

Take your time, enjoy, and eat less.

We think perfection is the key to better health — but it’s a trap.

In reality, avoiding apathy and embracing flexibility (and resilience) is a more effective path. https://t.co/tBxHJXcFS7

Suffering from mental fatigue? You might try to relax or watch a sitcom.

But your best bet might be movement medicine.

Currently on a 3-day fast liquid-only fast. 

Or, as my doctor called it, "food poisoning."

Everyone hypes the cold, but being hot has more interesting research. 

Studies suggest sauna could reduce your risk of fatal coronary heart disease up to 50 percent. 

And it could improve blood pressure and reduce all-cause mortality. 

The sweet spot: 15 min. a few times/week.

No “zero percent” weeks is the way. https://t.co/UgmfkGIrtY
Hat tip to @GidMK for a fantastic breakdown. When in doubt, don't panic when headlines make it seem like everything will kill you.

The headline: Splenda causes DNA damage!

The reality: If you eat 50,000 packets of Splenda or drink 10,000 cans of diet soda, then one biomarker of your DNA might be slightly elevated. 

Remember: health headlines are designed to grab attention, not necessarily reflect reality.

Can coffee be healthy and unhealthy?

It might depend on your preferred brew.

Research suggests unfiltered coffee (French Press & some cold brews) could raise LDL cholesterol.

Research is observational, but filtered coffee might be the best option for benefits and low risk.

Every diet wants to be considered a “lifestyle.”

But most fail because they don’t take the time to consider how you want to live. 

Finding a balance between good for you — and what you enjoy — is how you develop behaviors and habits that last.

What's the heaviest dumbbell you can pick up? It might tell you how well you're aging.

Research suggests grip strength is one of the best predictors of longevity.

A healthy grip is about 23 to 39 kg (50 to 85 pounds) for men and 16 to 22 kg (35 to 48 pounds) for women.
